speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Bluma Doman. Today's the 18th of February, 1996. The interview is being conducted in Johannesburg, South Africa, in English-- tape three. What was your initial reaction when you arrived at the Ozorkow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- we were heartbroken. And we couldn't believe that something like this is going to happen to us. We all got squashed together. And then at the end, they broke out typhoid fever, and a lot of young boys and girls died with typhoid fever. There was a big poverty. Very seldom you could see somebody which wasn't hungry. And we lived in ghetto, and there's only one room. My mother was heartbroken. She was always crying about my little brother, and so do we-- did we. But this was something. Food was very precious, very dear. You could buy from black market. Some even made it. They got out through the gate, and they brought in food, and they were selling it. So we would buy-- who had the money, you could buy. Who didn't, they was going around hungry. I remember families, which two children and a father, the mother was died. And one of the daughters was my girlfriend. What I saw, you can't believe it. In the wintertime, we were freezing. In the summertime, we scorched. Everyone in ghetto had packed their own duffel bag. We took out one piece. We washed it, we put it back. Because we will always knew that we'll be-- we-- you know-- running.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we've been in ghetto from 19-- the end of-- end-- very end of 1939. And we got chased into the ghetto in the beginning of 1940. We stayed in ghetto for two years. A lot of-- What were the guards doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were guards-- Germans. They guarded-- it was a-- a-- wires around, electric wires around the-- the ghetto. And they been guarding us with-- with big rifles. When we walked out to-- to-- to-- to work, they went with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Right in the beginning of the ghetto, by the entrance, they had a-- a police office. And there was some Jewish boys who also had a office, which had none our police, which they've been-- they've been elected to guard the people. There were no children anymore. All of them were gone, like I said, into Chelmno; and no elderly people, just middle-age and youth. We stayed till 1940 in ghetto. We had packed our-- our duffel bags, each for themself. And in 1940, they announced that we should go out in the outside the flats. And that's-- they put us in a big place, where there was a open field. And everybody had with them a-- a duffel bag. And it was always at-- at night. They did all the dirty work at night. They-- they came. They-- they-- the burgermeister, the German which my father sew to him-- was sewing for him. And he said, Sztorch, go and take what you want over here. As we saw, as the first people starting to leave the ghetto, to go to the trolley, all the-- the duffel bags were staying. They didn't let them take. As we saw this, I swear to you, my mother opened the duffel bag and puts on a pair of shoes. We came into Lodz, she had one red shoe and one black shoe. And we have to leave everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you to the Ozorkow ghetto in the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took a little furniture, which they-- were left, which the Poles even robbed too from us. [DRINKS] The main thing-- we had beds and bedding. And we didn't have much. The duffel bags were always packed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as we saw, then by transport they took us in groups at night in that streetcars to go to Lodz. It was on-- there was a blind-- there was a blind man. They shot him. And we-- they dragged him into the streetcar. And we came to Lodz-- they bury him.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a man, one was very sick. They went in. They shot him by the liquidation of the Ozorkow ghetto. And they knew there is a brother-in-law of his, so they sent the brother-in-law to bury his brother-in-law. We arrived to Lodz ghetto, 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you, were you able to keep any religious--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --articles with you, or follow any religious traditions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No religious. If somebody done, they had to hide themself. There was no-- there was nothing. In-- in Ozorkow ghetto, there was-- they made a hospital, because of the breakout of typhoid. But the doctors-- there were no doctors, no Jewish doctors. One was a intern, he wasn't yet a doctor. But he-- he was working that hospital that a lot of kids died over there-- young boys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we've been in Ozorkow ghetto. We've been to evacuate the ghetto. So it's 1940 in September. And we arrived to the ghetto, to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a big place. They call it in a-- in Poland, Platz Wolnosci. And there we're staying. Maybe it had Tadeusz Kosciuszko. He was a-- a very courageous soldier. So on this Platz Wolnosci, we're staying. And Kosciuszko's statue was staying. They didn't take it down yet. As we arrived, there was [NON-ENGLISH], our friend-- start talking to us about the zlotys you cannot spend here. Over here are rumkis. That's Rumkowski. They have special money for you. So if you want to make yourself easy, throw everything on the ground. You must hear my story of the young girl, but very courageous. My father had bought some Russian coins and gold during the war. And he said, if anything, if the war finish, if we have that money, we can always start anew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father, as we stay on that Platz, on that big, big, big place we're staying. My father, I had a-- I'll never forget it. I had a gray vest. My father had made me a waistcoat with little pockets, you know, like the men, with little pockets. He made me in the war. So they starting announcing, we see people throwing money, money out from their pockets. Me, I had in that vest, in that waistcoat some change. I threw it down. And all that money, which my father had bought, I pushed back to myself. And I had the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're coming into the-- to the ghetto in Lodz. My father start crying. I said, daddy, don't cry. I have all the money in my back. And he start-- he got hysterical, because I had the courage to do that. See, luckily, I had some change in my little pocket from that waistcoat. So I did save that. All right. We came into ghetto. They made, they call it [GERMAN] in German-- no food, no rations, no nothing till everything was going to be settled. The people which run the ghetto didn't even get the rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after-- what do you think? We've been sitting in one dinky room. The floor we used to take off to burn, to make fire. One room, three children, my parents, but we been all together. And they gave us some-- some bedding, because we didn't have nothing, and some beds. We had a little stove where my mother could cook.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summertime, it wasn't so bad. But in the wintertime, I had a pair of beautiful boots made in the ghetto in Ozorkow. So when my mother had to-- she didn't go to work. When my mother needed to go for the rations, I left the boots for her to wear then. I took her shoes to go to work, that she has worn on her feet. In the morning, before we left, my sister and myself, we brought water for her. We helped her as much-- [DRINKS] --as we can, that she stays in the house and cook that ration, and you may come home. In ghetto, we had after the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], they gave us carts. And we got food. But when we went in that [NON-ENGLISH], we were dying from hunger. So for a-- a coin-- a Russian coin, I went to the store. I got a bread. You know, this was a valuable-- the Russian coins in gold, you know, they are the best in the world. They have 18 carat gold. So 1/2 of the money was gone. And then we start walking to see some bedding. And then we sit-- we sit hungry. Finally, they assigned everybody to go to work. My father and my sister were assigned to one of the shops, a tailor shop also. And my-- she worked in my father's group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, I was assigned to a-- [NON-ENGLISH] resort, a shop for ladies. So I was assigned there. And my little brother was a little baby, he was 12 years old, they had-- Rumkowski was a big lover of children, you know? So they had a special shop. And my brother used to get special food there to eat, better than all of us. Thank God he's-- he's alive. So in ghetto it was very-- in Lodz ghetto it was very-- alive. I tell you, it can't be described. You-- people, 50 people a day were dying. They brought in Jews from Germany, from Hungary, from everywhere you could see in Lodz ghetto. A lot of them brought also money. What did they got for the money? Just as we got, for a coin, a bread. So I was assigned to a-- a ladies' shop. We worked over there from the morning. We got two soups a day and no bread-- we got in the ration. As we had the soups, my mother was already ill. So what did-- did I do? When I got the soup, I had with me-- you had to have your own dish and the own cup to get coffee in the morning. So I had that coffee cup. I took out all the potatoes, all the vegetables, and I put this for my-- to take home for my mother. My sister did the same thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day when we came home, we compared notes-- who got more. And then we put in some water, some salt. And we cook it, and my mother had a-- a good soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, who didn't work didn't get any rations, so my mother, why are we going to send her to work? She's-- she's-- she's heartbroken still about that little boy. And so there was a shop which started making knitted by machine, ear-muffins for the soldiers-- over the head. So we went to a factory, and we signed-- we signed our mother up. And we-- she everyday she went for her soup. And she got-- got a thing to sew. Who did sew? My sister and I. We did the sewing that she can go in the morning to the shop, bring her production, and she gets her soup. How old was your mother then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 48 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we are in ghetto for two years. You-- you meet people today-- even in the morning. We went to work. And you meet them today, feet like that, swollen, full of fluid. And I-- I met some people from our city, or even from Lodz. They said, oh, who are-- I said, how is he? He died. How is he? He died. Today you talk to them, tomorrow you find out he's dead, because there was no food, and the water accumulate. A lot of people died of tuberculosis. So now we are in ghetto. We're doing all the work for my mother. She always used to tell me-- to tell me, [NON-ENGLISH]. It means, you are a saint, what you're doing for me. I did more, because my sister was still young. And I was the oldest, so I felt I-- I had the responsibility. So all of a sudden, my mother took sick-- very ill. We went-- a doctor came to the office, to the house-- Jewish doctor. And he said my mother has fluid in her lungs. She-- she will not survive. So one day, I brought over a lady. I don't know if she was a nurse, but she did it. And she-- and she sucked out fluid from my mother's lungs-- yellow fluid. And it was TB. It was tuberculosis. I saw this water. I-- and she was sitting on her back. She was sitting, and the needle was here.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and myself, we ran to a store, and we bought her a apple to eat, to refresh herself. She was very weak after they did this, after they drew out the fluid. And she got worse and worse. In 1944, yeah, 1944, my brother was working in the children's shop. He's coming in. And he said that Rumkowski said that soon we're going to be evacuated from Lodz. So I don't know what happened. They postponed till six months. You should see my little brother when they announced this-- the evacuation is in six months. My brother jumped into the house through the window. And my mother was already sick. She was in bed. [DRINKS] He jumped through the window. He said, mommy, we're going to stay together. All right. Mommy, we stay together. Six months later, they start evacuating the Lodz ghetto. First, they took the old. There were no children in ghetto, but there were old people which they brought in from Hungary, from Germany, and from other countries. There was all nationalities you could take, but only Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and like I said, 1944, we-- they announced we're evacuating the ghetto. Where we're going, who knows? First, they took my sister and my father, because they worked in the same shop. And Sztorch and Sztorch, they figured it's relation, they should go. So then, they put-- my name wasn't there yet in mine father's shop. So my sister and my-- my father came into the house. And he said they stay on the list. They have to report to them tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was on her deathbed. I'll never forget this. My father, when he walked out with my sister, with the little duffel bag, with a few belongings, and he said to me, remember, take care of Mommy. And remember one thing. If you survive this war, everybody goes back to Ozorkow and waits on the step where we lived. And he walked out. A few days later, my mother died. The bullets were flying over my head. I couldn't go to the chevra kadisha to-- that's they bury mother. I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, in the middle of the night, I hear a lady is calling my mother's name. Who was it? A cousin with a-- with a friend, from Ozorkow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell them, you want to see Beila, Beila is here in bed. I don't know how to-- to go to the chevra kadisha. That's they bury her. She's laying in the bed in her clothes. She has to be washed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said, don't worry. Go out and put-- we put some water. We washed mama. And they washed her, and they dressed her in what we have, in some white shirts. And they put it with-- fit to a board-- from the floor we took off. We didn't have-- and two chairs. And they put my mommy's body. It was already three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do-- my brother doesn't lets me go, because he's clutching to me. Because he's-- that's all he has is me. So I said, look. We've got to bury mama. All right. Finally, he allowed me to go. The bullets were flying over our head. I ran to the chevra kadisha. And they took my mother. She's buried in-- in Lodz, in the cemetery, which we have recently a video. My son was so determined to find her grave and to put a stone. He spent a lot of money with the agency in Warsaw. But they can't find-- there-- there are graves-- we have the video-- a few weeks ago. There are graves, but none of them are marked. We've-- no. I'll finish with the ghetto. So now my brother and myself, my mother is gone already. I was relieved that she was buried. I was the oldest, my brother clutching to me. One-- one evening-- the Germans usually come in in the morning, hunting us, shoot, give shots, gun shots. And everybody had to go on to the yard. They didn't go yet to our yard.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they stop. 6 o'clock they went home for supper. And they never came back till next morning. So it was quiet. I said to my brother, we-- we are hungry. We sitting over here. We're starving. I'll go to the bakery-- run with my ration card. I'll have a-- take a bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, all right. But please come back. He stayed. It was just across the street, on [NON-ENGLISH] Street was that bakery. As we stay in line, and we're receiving the bread from the bakers, the Germans are here with-- with the big [NON-ENGLISH]. Me, clutching that bread-- it was hot, but I wouldn't give up that bread. As we walked on this side, as we walked, I saw somewhere a opening. I was courageous. Today I wouldn't do it. As I saw, I slipped into this house, and I walked up to the attic. And there laying a lot of more kids, people which they hide themselves. All right. There was a little window. And it was pitch dark. We decide we're going to come down. But the bread, I wouldn't give it up. I kept that bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear through that little windows, my brother's going, oh, they took my sister. What I'm going to do now? I'm only one in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was pitch dark. We walked down. And I have a bread. We sat down. We had something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my brother, listen. There is not many left now. We're almost in the last transport. Let's-- let's go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had our duffel bags. We went to the place where they collected all the Yidden to send to Auschwitz. We didn't know that we're going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, next to me, they loaded us on-- where the-- the pigs on the trains, where the pigs and the horses, where they'd been transferring-- loaded us like the herrings. It was quite a long journey to come to Auschwitz. And we sat in that-- in that-- street-- in that-- in those cars like the herring. And we volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you about the chevra kadisha activities in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very-- what could they do? They buried, I told you. They buried 50 people a day. What could they do? They had books. But we talked to this fellow, which is in charge of the Jewish cemetery in Lodz. A few months ago, my husband, my son, and myself, we talked to him. He said, I can't help you. There's no records of the last people. There are no marked graves. Everybody was buried, but the-- the-- the names are not put on the graves. So we have now the video.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your food rations, how big were the-- the slices of bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread, heavy, heavy with-- like-- like you say lame. You-- you making for-- to a bricks. And the bread was heavy. And you had to cut one bread for eight days-- no soap.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got potatoes in the ration. One-- one time we got a 1/2 a can of-- of meat. It was pig meat. Who cares what it is? We ate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went over at my mother. She said, I'll rather die that I should eat this. So I took the meat. And I went to a shop. They had little shops to eat in-- in ghetto. And I exchanged for beef. That time she ate. So that's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --that's how religious people were. You can see it. My mother said, I'd rather die that I should eat [NON-ENGLISH].
